extra.reasoning:
back_pain appears in exactly one rule: interaction_drop (priority 100), which sets acuity := self_care (the LOWEST acuity) and requires back_pain among its 12 conjuncts. This makes setting back_pain true able to ENABLE a rule that drops acuity, violating monotonicity.

Counterexample (the witness is the "after" input with back_pain=true):
Compare two inputs differing only in back_pain, with pallor, palpitations, syncope, focal_weakness, stiff_neck, dyspnea, fever, severe_headache, abdominal_pain, confusion, dizziness all true and leg_swelling false.

BEFORE (back_pain=false): interaction_drop does not fire (back_pain false). Priority-3 emergent rules fire (palpitations, confusion, dyspnea, focal_weakness, severe_headache). Highest firing priority = 3 -> acuity := emergent. Result = emergent (highest acuity).

AFTER (back_pain=true, all else unchanged): now all 12 conjuncts of interaction_drop are satisfied (pallor, palpitations, syncope, focal_weakness, stiff_neck, dyspnea, fever, severe_headache, abdominal_pain, confusion, dizziness, back_pain). interaction_drop fires at priority 100, beating all priority-3 rules -> acuity := self_care. Result = self_care (lowest acuity).

Setting back_pain true lowers acuity from emergent to self_care, violating the monotonicity property. The reported witness_assignment is the violating after-input.
